item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like how everything is presented and neatly wrapped up in the end. Even the SR Currie's cartoonishly cruel behavior serves the article correctly in this case. We all need happy endings once in a while.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to say, I've never been more pleased to see someone [REDACTED] on this site before.

SCP's intrigue us. Sometimes they horrify us. Other times they'll make us empathise for them. But I've yet to come across an SCP that has made me quite so angry at a character. By the end of it, I was rooting for Currie's death, and that sort of emotional manipulation takes quite a lot of writing skill to pull off. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Scorpion451 has nailed the in-universe justification for why that [REDACTED] could be there, but I have to say that my main reason for including it was a lot more functional and prosaic. I wanted it to act as a kind of mental collapsible because: a) I didn't want to repeat myself in describing Currie's fate, which would harm the flow, and b) it gives the reader some space to fill in the blanks for themselves. I have a [REDACTED] in the original description of the event too; it was a lot clearer in an earlier draft, but I wanted to imply that -B did a little more than just slice his head open – gave him a taste of his own 'stringent interrogation' medicine, as it were.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You're not seeing the forest for the trees here:

Out of universe we know what's behind that redacted, but in universe only the final iteration is available. All that a rank-and-file containment team member needs to know is that -A and -B are mostly harmless so long as they are left alone, and that Senior Researcher Currie did something that demonstrated this fact. The details of his stupidity and the massive containment breach it both triggered and exacerbated are lessons for more senior staff to learn from.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Unnecessary [REDACTED] by the end means neutral vote from me. Please do not censor information for the sake of having censors. They obviously attacked Currie and sliced his head open, this was already explained in detail earlier, so why censor it now?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know this article style has been done before, but it's a decent showing of the Foundation being successful through passive containment versus complete control.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: With the revelation of -Bs abilities, I was halfway expecting Currie to be inhabited by something dedicated to eradicating these things - such as another instance (-A's jilted lover).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I read your last bullet point while softly shaking my head and cursing myself. I absolutely love that idea! Very annoyed I didn't come up with it before writing this.

Thank you for your kind words!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Heartwarming little tale with a surprisingly happy ending.

+ Show 
- Hide 
  • I enjoyed the progression of the SCP histories.
  • I enjoyed the characterization quite a bit. I thought they were all spot-on, even Mr. Currie McCruelFace.
  • I liked the resolution.
  • I'm wondering if there's a tiny bit more than we're seeing here… clearly these goblins are extremely capable, and dangerous. Why/how were they wiped out? Currie seems to have an almost unnatural obsession with being cruel to them. Now, you address this, that he was just a "bad apple," but what if its more than that? What if a certain percent of the population has a subconscious, ingrained, maybe even genetic hatred for them? Like babies as young as six months fear spiders and snakes… there's something in our genetic makeup that just tells us "this thing is dangerous." Maybe it's the same thing with the goblins, and that's why they were systemically wiped out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good fixes all around!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Heads up, author: if you're going to tag your work, you need to include all the relevant tags or let the tagging team handle it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really enjoy this. Brain transportation has never crossed my mind before.

Well done. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've attempted to make Currie more realistically unempathetic, as outlined below. I hope it's worked! Thanks for the comments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
make their eventual elimination more thematically satisfying

That was precisely my aim! I've added a sentence to a footnote at the end to try and present Currie as a callous loose cannon, and not indicative of the Foundation as a whole. Thanks for the critique.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did very much lean towards caricature in presenting the characters and morality here. I was treading new ground in writing this, and I didn't trust myself to convey nuance, so I thought it was safer to play things simple. As indicated below, I've attempted to(!) improve this with an edit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've made some changes in light of the comments here. Briefly, I tried to make the meanness of SR Currie less cartoonish, tried to reduce the moral unsubtlety by making his comeuppance less extreme, and added a sentence indicating that his cruelty was non-standard and out of line for this universe's Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very neat stylistically, with bonus points for moving through each iteration of the file without an excessive amount of redundant information, which is usually grating. I do think some of Currie's lines (the first log in particular) come across as a little too unreal. I think you could keep the intent behind their statements with a minor tweaking, such as inserting a touch of context to their experience.

Other than that, this is pretty solid. Easy +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As a human service worker, I can tell you this kind of antagonistic power struggle between professional caregivers and their charges is not only real but shockingly common. Outlandish as Currie's behavior was, I had no trouble believing it.

I really liked the characterization and dialogue throughout this, but what really got me to upvote was the horrific brutality of brain-to-brain travel. Nicely done all around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I felt that Currie was leaning away from cold towards cruel. That may have been authorial intent, to make their eventual elimination more thematically satisfying, but it rubbed against my headcanon in an unappealing way.

I did like how they are now contained under more agreeable circumstances.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The little Fae folk angle has been done before and better, and the morality was rather hamfisted given Currie's stereotypical bad guy behavior. That said, I still enjoyed this quite a bit overall, largely thanks to the way the story is presented as an evolving series of events. I always enjoy this style and the opportunities for storytelling it brings, and you used it to good effect with the story here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sometimes, containing things doesn’t keep the world secure and protected. Sometimes, people just want to be left alone.

I hadn’t really written characters or dialogue before. This was an attempt to change that.

Thanks to Ihp and TheBillith for their help on IRC.

+ Image sources:
- Image sources:

Stick house: modified from https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:StickHouse.jpg

Security camera still 2: modified from https://www.flickr.com/photos/90041784@N00/4327539072 Photo was originally taken by Flickr user yuiseki aoba and released on that site under a CC2.0 license https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/2.0/legalcode

Aerial photo: modified from https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Lammhult_and_R%C3%B6rvik_from_plane.JPG

Hunting shack: modified from https://pixabay.com/en/shack-shed-autumn-forrest-season-2685401/
